text:
English Wielisc, Wylisc (West Saxon), Welisc, Wælisc (Anglian Kentish) "foreign; British (not Anglo-Saxon), Welsh; free, servile," from Wealh, Walh "Celt, Briton, Welshman, non-Germanic foreigner;" Tolkien's definition, "common Gmc.
name what should call Celtic speech," also applied Germanic languages speakers Latin, hence High German Walh, Walah "Celt, Roman, Gaulish," Norse Val-land "France," Valir "Gauls, non-Germanic inhabitants France" (Danish vælsk "Italian, French, southern"); from Proto-Germanic *Walkhiskaz, from Celtic tribal name represented Latin Volcæ (Caesar) "ancient Celtic tribe southern Gaul."
noun, "the Britons," also "the Welsh language," both from English.
Among English, Welsh used disparagingly inferior substitute things (such Welsh cricket "louse" (1590s); Welsh comb "thumb four fingers" (1796), compare welch (v.)).
Welsh rabbit from 1725, also perverted folk-etymology Welsh rarebit (1785).
